section.title: The Flood
section.sectionRationale: The judgment announced now falls. Noah and the living cargo enter the ark, the LORD Himself shuts the door, and the fountains of the deep and the floodgates of heaven open until all flesh perishes. The chapter is a single sustained account, from the final summons into the ark to the waters prevailing for a hundred and fifty days, holding together deadly judgment and sheltered salvation.
section.thesis: As the waters of judgment undo the world, God shuts His people safely inside the ark, so that death outside and salvation inside flow from the same sovereign hand.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the final summons into the ark; the gathering and entering; the deep breaking open and the LORD shutting the door; and the waters prevailing over all flesh.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Noah, “Go into the ark, you and all your family, because I have found you righteous in this generation.
You are to take with you seven pairs of every kind of {{clean|clean}} animal, a male and its mate; a pair of every kind of unclean animal, a male and its mate;
and seven pairs of every kind of bird of the air, male and female, to preserve their offspring on the face of all the earth.
For seven days from now I will send rain on the earth for forty days and forty nights, and I will wipe from the face of the earth every living thing I have made.”
And Noah did all that the LORD had commanded him.
Now Noah was 600 years old when the floodwaters came upon the earth.
And Noah and his wife, with his sons and their wives, entered the ark to escape the waters of the flood.
The clean and unclean animals, the birds, and everything that crawls along the ground
came to Noah to enter the ark, two by two, male and female, as God had commanded Noah.
And after seven days the floodwaters came upon the earth.

units.label: The deep breaks open, the LORD shuts the door

verses.text: In the six hundredth year of Noah’s life, on the seventeenth day of the second month, all the fountains of the great {{deep|deep}} burst forth, and the floodgates of the heavens were opened.
And the rain fell upon the earth for forty days and forty nights.
On that very day Noah entered the ark, along with his sons Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and his wife, and the three wives of his sons,
they and every kind of wild animal, livestock, crawling creature, bird, and winged creature.
They came to Noah to enter the ark, two by two of every creature with the breath of life.
And they entered, the male and female of every living thing, as God had commanded Noah. Then the LORD {{shut|shut him in}}.
For forty days the flood kept coming on the earth, and the waters rose and lifted the ark high above the earth.
So the waters continued to surge and rise greatly on the earth, and the ark floated on the surface of the waters.
Finally, the waters completely prevailed upon the earth, so that all the high mountains under all the heavens were covered.
The waters rose and covered the mountaintops to a depth of fifteen cubits.
And every living thing that moved upon the earth perished, birds, livestock, animals, every creature that swarms upon the earth, and all mankind.
Of all that was on dry land, everything that had the breath of life in its nostrils died.

And every living thing on the face of the earth was {{blotted|blotted out}}, man and livestock, crawling creatures and birds of the air; they were blotted out from the earth, and only Noah and those with him in the ark remained.
And the waters prevailed upon the earth for 150 days.

clean.gloss: Clean, pure, fit for worship. Extra clean animals are brought aboard, seven pairs, so that after the flood there will be animals to sacrifice, as Noah does in 8:20. The categories of clean and unclean, later spelled out in the law, are already present, showing the worship of God in view even in the ark.

deep.gloss: The deep, the primeval waters. The same tehom that covered the earth before creation in 1:2 now bursts up from below while the heavens open above. The flood is a kind of un-creation, the waters God once separated and bounded rushing back to undo a world given over to corruption.

shut.gloss: To shut, close, enclose. Then the LORD shut him in. God Himself seals the door of the ark, both sealing out the waters of judgment and sealing in those He saves. It is a picture of divine keeping; the safety of the rescued rests on God's hand, not the strength of the latch.

blotted.gloss: To wipe out, blot out, erase. Every living thing was blotted out from the earth. The word pictures wiping a surface clean; God erases a corrupted world. Yet the same God who blots out sinners can also blot out sins, and Scripture later pleads and promises exactly that, Psalm 51:1, Isaiah 43:25.

basic.context: The judgment God warned about now comes. Noah, his family, and the animals go into the ark, and then the waters break loose from below and above until everything living on the land dies. Only those in the ark survive. A striking moment is that the LORD Himself shuts the door. The question is how judgment and salvation can come together.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, and the account is careful and detailed, with dates and measurements. People debate how far the flood reached, but the main message is plain: God judged a corrupt world, and He saved those He had sheltered. God's own act of shutting the door shows that salvation is His work.
basic.originalLanguages: Extra clean, tahor, animals come aboard so there will be some to sacrifice later. The fountains of the deep, tehom, the same waters as at creation, burst up while the heavens open, a kind of un-creation. The LORD shuts, sagar, Noah in. And every living thing is blotted out, machah, the same word later used for God blotting out sins.
basic.typology: The ark carrying its people safely through the flood pictures salvation in Christ, and Peter links it to baptism. The LORD shutting the door pictures God's sure keeping of His people. The un-creation by water points ahead to a coming judgment and a new creation on the far side.
basic.christ: The ark that carries Noah safely through the waters of death points to Jesus, in whom His people pass through judgment unharmed. The LORD who shut the door is the God who keeps His own secure in Christ, from whom nothing can separate them.
basic.god: God is the sovereign Judge who brings the flood, and the sovereign Saviour who shuts His people safely inside. Both the death outside and the rescue inside come from His hand. He is faithful to His warning and faithful to His promise, and He keeps those He saves.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 24 uses the flood as a warning of the coming judgment. 1 Peter 3 links the ark to baptism and salvation. 2 Peter 3 compares the flood to the coming judgment by fire. Psalm 51 asks God to blot out sin.
basic.oneStory: The waters of judgment undo a corrupt world, but God shelters a remnant in the ark and shuts them safely in. Judgment and salvation flow from the same hand. This great rescue through the waters carries the promise forward and foreshadows the final judgment and the sure salvation God gives in Christ.

context.literary: A carefully structured account marked by precise dates, ages, and measurements, and by repeated notes that Noah did as God commanded. It builds through entering, the breaking open of the deep, and the rising waters, with the LORD shutting the door as the still center of the storm.
context.historical: The dating by Noah's age and the calendar, and the language of the great deep and floodgates, reflect the account's claim to be sober history. For Israel it declared that the God of the covenant is Lord over the waters of chaos and over life and death.
context.canonical: A pattern of judgment and salvation echoed at the Red Sea and in baptism, and read by the New Testament as a preview of the final judgment. The un-creation here anticipates the promise of a new heavens and new earth.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the coming of the flood as righteous judgment on a corrupt world and as the salvation of Noah's household, stressing God's sovereignty in both the destruction outside and the keeping of those inside.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account describes God's judgment and rescue; it does not command us to reenact them. Its abiding truths are the certainty of judgment, the reality of salvation by God's provision, and the security of those God keeps.

debates.question: How far did the flood extend?

views.label: A global flood covering the whole earth
views.case: The language is sweeping: all the high mountains under all the heavens were covered, every living thing on dry land died, and only those in the ark remained; the plain sense and the scale of the ark point to a worldwide deluge.

views.label: A vast regional flood filling the known world
views.case: Hebrew phrases for all the earth can mean the whole known land, and some read the account as describing a catastrophic flood over the whole world humanity then inhabited, without settling every geological question.

debates.bearing: The passage's theological claim holds on either reading: God really judged the world of that day for its corruption and really saved all who were with Noah in the ark.

study.originalLanguages: The account trades on words loaded with meaning. Clean animals, tahor, are gathered in extra number, already looking ahead to worship and sacrifice after the flood. The catastrophe itself is described as an un-creation: the fountains of the great tehom, the deep, the same primeval waters of 1:2, burst up from below while the floodgates of heaven open above, and the waters God once bounded rush back over the world. At the eye of the storm the LORD sagar, shuts, Noah in, sealing out judgment and sealing in salvation by His own hand. And outside, every living thing is machah, blotted out, wiped from the earth, the very verb the Psalms will later seize when they beg God to blot out sin instead of the sinner.

typology.type: The ark bearing its people through the flood
typology.fulfillment: Salvation in Christ, through whom believers pass safely through judgment; explicitly tied to baptism, 1 Peter 3:20-21.

typology.type: The LORD shutting the door of the ark
typology.fulfillment: God's sure keeping of His people in Christ, from whom nothing can separate them, John 10:28-29, Romans 8:38-39.

typology.type: The un-creation by the waters of the deep
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment and the promise of a renewed creation on the far side of it, 2 Peter 3:5-13.

study.christ: The flood account preaches Christ in shadow. The ark that carries Noah, his family, and the animals safely through the very waters that destroy the world pictures Jesus, in whom God's people pass through judgment untouched; Peter draws the line directly to the salvation we have in Christ, symbolized in baptism. The most tender detail, the LORD shut him in, points to the God who keeps His own secure; those sealed inside the ark are safe not because of the strength of the door but because God closed it, just as those who are in Christ are held so fast that nothing can separate them from His love. And the un-creation by water anticipates the day the Son of Man will judge the world and bring the new creation He has secured.
study.god: God stands over this chapter as both Judge and Saviour. He brings the flood, opening the deep and the heavens; the destruction is His righteous act against a world wholly corrupt. Yet the same hand that unleashes the waters shuts the door of the ark, and the note that the LORD shut him in reveals a God who personally secures His people. Death outside the ark and life inside it both flow from His sovereignty. He is faithful to His warning and faithful to His promise, and those He saves He also keeps.

crossReferences.note: People ate and drank until the flood came and swept them all away; so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
Eight persons were saved through water in the ark, which prefigures the baptism that saves through Christ's resurrection.
The world was deluged and destroyed by water; the present heavens and earth are reserved for judgment by fire.
Blot out my transgressions, using the same verb by which every living thing was blotted out in the flood.

study.oneStory: The waters of the deep, once bounded at creation, now rush back to un-make a corrupted world. Yet God shelters a remnant in the ark and shuts them safely in with His own hand, so that judgment outside and salvation inside come from the same sovereign God. This rescue through the waters carries the promise forward and casts a long shadow toward the final judgment and the sure, kept salvation God gives in Christ.

quiz.q: What tender detail shows that salvation is God's own work in this chapter?

options.text: The LORD Himself shut Noah in, sealing the door of the ark.
options.note: God personally secures His people inside the ark.

options.text: Noah bolted the door firmly from the inside.
options.note: The text says the LORD shut him in, not Noah; the keeping is God's.

options.text: The animals sealed themselves inside.
options.note: It was the LORD who shut the door, highlighting divine keeping.

options.text: The door was left open the whole time.
options.note: The LORD shut the door before the waters prevailed.

quiz.q: Why were extra clean animals brought onto the ark?

options.text: So that after the flood there would be animals for sacrifice and worship.
options.note: Noah offers clean animals in worship right after the flood in 8:20.

options.text: Because clean animals were simply prettier.
options.note: The distinction is about worship and sacrifice, not appearance.

options.text: Because unclean animals were not allowed on the ark.
options.note: Unclean animals were also brought, just in smaller number.

options.text: For no particular reason.
options.note: The extra clean animals look ahead to post-flood sacrifice.

quiz.q: How does the passage describe the flood as a kind of un-creation?

options.text: The fountains of the great deep burst forth and the floodgates of heaven opened, the bounded waters rushing back.
options.note: The primeval waters of creation return to undo a corrupt world.

options.text: The sun and moon fell from the sky.
options.note: The text speaks of the deep and the floodgates, not the sun and moon.

options.text: The dry land simply cracked apart.
options.note: The imagery is of waters from below and above, undoing the separation of creation.

options.text: Nothing about creation is echoed at all.
options.note: The great deep deliberately echoes the waters of Genesis 1.

quiz.q: How does the ark point forward to salvation in Christ?

options.text: Those inside pass safely through the waters of judgment, as believers are saved through Christ.
options.note: 1 Peter 3 ties the ark's rescue to baptism and salvation in Christ.

options.text: It teaches that only eight people can ever be saved.
options.note: The number is historical; the ark pictures salvation for all God brings in.

options.text: It shows that we save ourselves by our own effort.
options.note: God provides and seals the rescue; it is His work, not self-salvation.

options.text: The ark has no bearing on salvation.
options.note: The New Testament reads the ark as a picture of salvation in Christ.

quiz.q: Why can the extent of the flood be held with some humility?

options.text: Serious readers differ between a global and a vast regional flood, yet the theological point stands either way.
options.note: God truly judged that world and truly saved those in the ark on either view.

options.text: Because the Bible never describes the flood's reach at all.
options.note: The text does use sweeping language; the debate is how to read it, not whether it speaks.

options.text: Because the answer decides whether God exists.
options.note: The reality of God and His judgment does not hinge on this detail.

options.text: Because the flood was clearly only a local rain shower.
options.note: The account describes a catastrophic, world-covering judgment, not a passing shower.

questions: The LORD Himself shut the door of the ark. How does it steady you to know that your safety rests on God's keeping hand rather than on the strength of your own grip?

Outside the ark was judgment, inside was shelter, and the difference was God's provision. Where in your life are you tempted to build your own shelter instead of taking refuge in the one God provides?
Noah did all that the LORD commanded, then waited seven days as the world carried on as normal. How do you keep trusting God's word in the gap between His command and the visible outcome?
The word for blotting out the world is the same word the Psalms use for God blotting out sin. How does it move you that the God who judges sin also offers to wipe your sins away in Christ?
Jesus said the days of Noah picture the world before His return. What would it look like to live ready, rather than asleep, in a world going on as if judgment will never come?

translationNotes.note: Seven pairs of every kind of clean animal can also be rendered seven of every kind of clean animal; either way the clean animals are taken in greater number, providing for the sacrifices Noah offers after the flood.

The fountains of the great deep uses the word tehom, the same deep that covered the earth in 1:2; the flood is portrayed as the bounded waters of creation returning, a deliberate picture of un-creation.
